data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the newest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

It was a tough five days for Wall Street, as U.S. markets on Friday slumped to their worst week since late May. The decline was primarily driven by a sharp sell-off on Friday, triggered by the resurgence of trade war concerns.

Heading into Friday, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was actually positive for the week and was fresh off another record close, achieved on Wednesday. Market participants had largely shrugged off the continued U.S. government shutdown, and the lack of economic data had not appeared to faze traders. Precious metals grabbed the spotlight from equities, with spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) surpassing the historic $4,000/oz level and spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) scaling the $50/oz mark for the first time ever.

However, on Friday, sentiment was rocked after President Donald Trump accused China of "hostile" export controls on rare earths and said the U.S. would impose an additional 100% tariff on products from the Asian nation. The S&P 500 (SP500) and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) posted their worst day since early April in response.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slid -2.4%, while the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) retreated -2.5%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) cratered -2.7%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Berkshire (BRK.B) Should Acquire All Of Kraft Heinz (KHC).

BND - Earnings Yields, Rate Cuts, And Mean Reversion Point.

CoreWeave's (CRWV) Back With The Bulls After Securing Deals.

The Opportunity I Waited For - Why I'm Buying Ferrari (RACE).

Great Elm Capital's (GECC) Dramatic Drop Presents Opportunity.

I'm A Bit Cautious On UiPath (PATH) Till Key Metrics Stabilize.

Petrobras (PBR) Is Cheap For A Reason - Politics Back In Focus.

Wait And See: SMH's Peaking Volumes Imply Potential Implosion.

SoundHound (SOUN): Strong Execution, But Its Valuation Is Wild.

PepsiCo's (PEP) EPS Beat Due To Price Hikes, Not Volume Growth.

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -2.7% to 45,480. S&P 500 -2.4% to 6,553. Nasdaq -2.5% to 22,204. Russell 2000 -3.3% to 2,395. CBOE Volatility Index +30.1% to 21.66.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples +0.6%. Utilities +1.4%. Financials -3%. Telecom -2.2%. Healthcare -1.9%. Industrials -2.9%. Information Technology -2.5%. Materials -3.1%. Energy -4%. Consumer Discretionary -3.3%. Real Estate -3.3%.

World Indices
London -0.7% to 9,427. France -2.0% to 7,918. Germany -0.6% to 24,241. Japan +5.1% to 48,089. China +0.4% to 3,897. Hong Kong -3.1% to 26,290. India +1.6% to 82,501.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI -3.3% to $58.9/bbl. Gold +2.3% to $4,000.4/oz. Natural Gas -6.6% to 3.106. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 4.051.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD -1.05%. USD/JPY +2.53%. GBP/USD -0.89%. Bitcoin -8.2%. Litecoin -18.4%. Ethereum -15.8%. XRP -19.9%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +31%. Dell Technologies (DELL) +7%. Arista Networks (ANET) +6%. Netflix (NFLX) +6%. PepsiCo (PEP) +6%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
AppLovin (APP) -17%. Seagate Technology Holdings plc (STX) -15%. Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) -15%. D.R. Horton (DHI) -13%. Dow (DOW) -13%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: Icahn Enterprises: Troubles Mount, 28% Yield Likely To Go Poof
data.description:
On our last coverage of Icahn Enterprises L.P. (NASDAQ:IEP), we downgraded the stock back to a "Strong Sell." In doing so, we recognized that the setup was once again compelling for the downside.

While the price has not increased much, the 200-day moving average has caught up. The price now sits just 5% below it. The NAV per unit has also dropped appreciably in this timeframe as the stock has moved higher. In other words, the premium has increased significantly. We think the next down leg is now very close.

Source: Next Leg Down Should Be Close For This 21% Yielder (Rating Downgrade)

The stock has underperformed the broader market by about 10% since then.

We go over what has happened since our last update and tell you why the risks have actually gone up for this one, despite the stock going lower.

The Thing That Helped

IEP's large stake in CVR Energy (CVI) is no secret. There have been many attempts by investors to gauge the current NAV using movements in CVI. CVI also was a key reason that we did not put a sell rating in December 2024. CVI was oversold and looked a bit undervalued.

Since then the stock has had a fantastic rally.

What has driven this scintillating performance? The short answer: The market has gone completely bonkers. The slightly longer answer from the legendary Liz Ann Sonders.

Yes, the worse the fundamentals, the better the year-to-date performance has been. CVI is matching that pattern.

Not only had it rallied in the face of such losses, but it has done so even as the forward estimates have also been crushed in the last 3 months.

CVI is now extremely expensive, even using price to sales, which strips out cyclicality.

This was the same ratio that told us not to be bearish in December 2024 when everyone was worried about earnings. So should CVI reverse course, as we expect, things will flow to its largest holder, IEP.

What Has Not Worked

While CVI has been a bullish wind beneath the injured IEP, everything else has steadily deteriorated. The Q2-205 report showed once again that IEP is not able to turn a profit even under good circumstances.

More importantly, cash drain remains incredibly high for the firm.

The cash on the balance sheet has withered over the last 12 months.

While investors might get excited at the prospect of $4.476 billion of cash, they need to balance that with the total liabilities.

In fact, while IEP reported a higher total NAV (total NAV, not NAV per unit), the market was extremely skeptical of its prospects. The most recent bond sale shows just how bad things are for IEP.

Icahn Enterprises together with Icahn Enterprises Finance, plans to commence an offering of additional $500M of 10.000% Senior Secured Notes due 2029.The net proceeds from the offering will be used, together with cash on hand, to partially redeem the issuers' existing 6.250% Senior Notes due 2026.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Since this ball was set rolling in August 2025, the impact wasn't seen in Q2-2025 and won't be fully felt till Q4-2025. The company's two near term bonds (2026 and 2027) look well bid with sub-6.5% yields to maturity. Beyond that, the street is worried.

We will add here that this is during a time of absolute disregard for risk in the markets. High-yield spreads are the tightest since (checks notes) 1998. IEP rated B1 is yielding more on the long end than most C- rated credits.

Verdict

Yeah that yield looks enticing if you can actually capture it.

But no one has really captured it, at least not recently. The stock's depreciation has far exceeded the extraordinary yield.

As things stand, the stock remains extremely expensive even relative to its own optimistic NAV. The reason is, of course, the dilution from issuing units to pay the distribution. This chart looks so extreme that one wonders why investors have not dumped this en masse to actually push it below the NAV.

Current NAV per unit is around $5.75, and that is our price objective over the next few months. The best bet for IEP is actually a very large equity offering to reduce debt. The stock trades well above its own NAV and should be used as a currency to help things. Of course, doing so and then cutting the distribution to zero might run into severe problems. So the course being followed is this indirect dilution. That really doesn't help, as it is only reducing cash drain from distributions versus creating an excess that could be used to pay debt. We are maintaining a "strong sell" and also looking for opportunities to short CVI should it spike higher.

data.title: URTY And IWM: A Bet On Russell 2000 With Short, Medium And Long-Term Catalysts
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, the ProShares UltraPro Russell2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:URTY) has performed in line with the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM), delivering a gain of ~12%, albeit both ETFs have marginally underperformed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY):

Looking at the snippet above, we observe that URTY dramatically underperformed during the April 2025 market volatility, although the recent recovery has helped it claw back these losses.

I rate both URTY and IWM with a Buy rating, with my investment thesis encompassing long, medium-, and long-term catalysts, including:

  • A resolution to the U.S. government shutdown and two 0.25% Fed rate cuts by the end of 2025.
  • An additional two 0.25% rate cuts in 2026 and a focus on economic resilience and deregulation by President Trump and the Republicans going into the midterm elections.
  • Rerating higher of small capitalization stocks given the significant valuation discount they trade at relative to large-cap peers.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks to the investment case.

ETFs Overview

You can access all URTY details on the ProShares website here. The ProShares UltraPro Russell2000 ETF seeks to deliver a 300% (3x) return of the small-cap focused Russell 2000 index. This makes the ETF's returns more dependent on factors driving the U.S. economy as opposed to the more internationally-oriented S&P 500.

I should note that some data on the ProShares website is only updated quarterly. As of the time of writing, the latest data is from H1 2025, hence somewhat outdated. As such, for more up-to-date information on the Russell 2000 index, you may want to review the IWM website here. In essence, both ETFs track the same underlying index, with the key difference being that IWM uses no leverage while URTY provides 300% leverage on a daily basis.

URTY's net expense ratio stands at 0.95%, largely in line with other 300% ETFs, but notably above the 0.19% expense ratio for IWM. When talking with friends about investments, I often recommend ETFs with 0.1-0.2% expense ratios for long-term passive holdings. Hence, URTY's elevated expense ratio makes it more of a trading tool for risk-seeking investors.

In contrast, IWM's 0.19% expense ratio is only marginally above the cheapest options for U.S. stock market exposure, making IWM a solid long-term hold for more conservative passive investors.

Short-Term Catalysts

In the very near term, a potential resolution of the ongoing government shutdown could provide a tailwind for small-cap U.S. stocks as it will remove uncertainty from the economic outlook.

Fed funds futures price in two more 0.25% rate cuts by the end of 2025. While a prolonged government shutdown will be a clear negative for IWM and URTY, it may also frontload Fed easing as the focus will quickly shift from inflation to shaky labor market fundamentals.

Fed easing will be more beneficial to Russell 2000 components (as opposed to S&P 500 constituents) due to the domestic-focused nature of the index and the elevated use of floating-rate debt by small-capitalization companies.

Medium-Term Catalysts

Looking ahead to 2026, Fed funds futures are pricing two more 0.25% rate cuts in 2026, ultimately taking the Fed funds rate to about 3% in December 2026:

As such, small-cap Russell 2000 components should continue to benefit from lower funding costs.

The other key event in the year will be the November 3, 2026, midterm elections. I believe President Trump and the Republicans will try to stabilize the U.S. economy going into the polls. As such, I do not expect major policy announcements such as new tariffs, which may bring new uncertainty into the domestic U.S. economy. Instead, I expect elevated fiscal stimulus and progress on deregulation, which may be a boon for small-cap Russell 2000 components.

Long-Term Catalysts

The past decade has seen the SPY dramatically outperform both IWM and URTY, although we do see URTY outperforming during strong bull markets in 2016-2017 and post-COVID-19 2020-2021:

This has resulted in IWM trading at 18.3x its trailing earnings, a significant discount relative to the 28.9x trailing earnings multiple for the S&P 500. While valuations do not matter in the short term, in the long term, I believe they are a key driving factor (along with earnings growth) for stock returns. As such, I think the circa 37% trailing earnings yield discount small-caps trade at is excessive.

It is true that some of the discount can be explained by the Russell 2000's overweight position in cyclical sectors such as industrials and financials, at 17.85% and 17.51% of IWM assets:

Still, I believe that the valuation gap will close one way or another, with the bullish outlook being that IWM components will rerate higher to large-caps, while the bearish outlook envisages SPY constituents rerating lower.

URTY And IWM ratings

I believe the choice between IWM and URTY depends on your conviction in the short, medium, and long-term catalysts outlined above. I believe both ETFs are worth a Buy rating, with URTY likely to outperform in the event of a quick resolution of the government shutdown and fast Fed rate cuts.

IWM will likely outperform in the event of elevated near- and medium-term volatility (which will increase the drag on URTY returns from beta slippage). IWM will also outperform if large-cap U.S. stocks decline in value, which will reduce the appeal of going long small-cap Russell 2000 constituents.

As always, the choice between the two ETFs has its pros and cons, largely dependent on individual investor risk tolerance and investment objectives.

Risks

I would also divide risks into three groups. Over the short term, a prolonged government shutdown will weigh on IWM and URTY returns, with URTY the clear ETF to avoid if you see no quick resolution to the current situation.

Over the medium term, the Fed may also cut rates more slowly than currently expected, reducing the earnings tailwind from lower interest rates which will disproportionately favor small-capitalization stocks.

U.S. economic weakness may prove longer lasting, potentially resulting in a recession, which will heavily impact URTY and IWM returns given the overweight position in cyclical sectors the Russell 2000 index currently exhibits.

Over the long term, a higher valuation for U.S. large-caps may be justified by outsized growth stemming from artificial intelligence, while small cap companies may end up being disproportionately disrupted by significant changes in society and the economy.

Investors who cannot bear the volatility associated with URTY's performance but still want to capture the upside offered by the Russell 2000 over the long term should consider the unleveraged IWM ETF. You can read more about the risk-return profile and risks associated with leveraged ETFs on the SEC website here.

Conclusion

URTY and IWM have marginally underperformed the SPY so far in 2025, in line with the poor returns delivered over the past decade. Looking ahead, I see three types of catalysts for URTY and IWM, ranking both ETFs with a Buy rating:

  • Short-term catalysts include a swift resolution to the U.S. government shutdown and two 0.25% Fed rate cuts by the end of 2025.
  • Medium-term catalysts include an additional two 0.25% rate cuts in 2026 and a focus on economic resilience and deregulation by President Trump and the Republicans going into the midterm elections.
  • Long-term catalysts include a significant valuation discount small capitalization stocks trade at relative to large-cap peers.

The choice between the two ETFs will naturally depend on the individual investor's conviction in the catalysts outlined above, as well as personal risk tolerance.

Investors should consider risks stemming from a prolonged government shutdown, slower Fed rate cuts, a potential U.S. recession, and the possibility of large-cap valuations rerating lower to small cap peers.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: TSMC: Ultra Bullish Going Into Q3 Earnings
data.description:
After my last coverage on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM), the stock is up by double digits. I believe my ratings on this stock are spot on, with the share price doubling since I started covering this stock back in April.

Q3 earnings are right around the corner (October 16), so I decided to revisit my bull case to see if anything had changed. Given that the last coverage focused on TSMC's upside from a (likely) B30A chip to China, in this piece, I considered covering some of the catalysts that could materialize next year, with the N2 and A16 process nodes at the forefront of the technology roadmap of the company.

Overall, I remain ultra bullish on this stock, and I still see a cheap company on a valuation basis despite the price surge since April this year.

Strong Top-Line Growth in Q3 2025

As you may know, TSMC publishes monthly consolidated net revenue on its investor site, typically around the 8th–10th of the following month. The data for July, August, and September looks particularly favorable. Below is a compilation of the top-line results:

  • July 2025: NT$323.17B, representing a +22.5% m/m and +25.8% y/y increase.
  • August 2025: NT$335.77B, representing a +3.9% m/m and +33.8% y/y increase.
  • September 2025: NT$330.98B, down 1.4% m/m and up 31.4% y/y.

In the past earnings call, management raised the outlook for 2025 on the top line, with an expected 30% yoy growth (in USD). Q3 revenue was guided in the range of $31.8 - $33.0B, representing an 8% sequential increase and 38% y/y increase at midpoint.

The eyes are still on the AI/HPC demand acceleration. In the past quarter, the HPC platform represented 60% of the total revenue, with sales growth ticking up by 14% sequentially.

On the bottom line, the overseas fabs are leading to a multi-year gross margin dilution. I already discussed this topic in detail in my last article and flagged it as a risk to my bull case.

Looking at the Q3 2025 earnings, I believe the market will pay close attention to how gross margins are evolving. The expectation from management is a compression in gross margins by 210 bps in Q3. I don't think results would come lower than that, as N3 capacity is very tight, and it seems to remain the same for the next couple of years, as noted by CEO C. C. Wei during the last earnings call:

N3 capacity. It will be continued for a couple of years, very tight. And in fact, N5 also very tight. The demand is high because of a lot of AI products still in the 4-nanometer technology node, and they will transition to 3-nanometer probably in next 2 years. So in meanwhile, N5 are still very tight in capacity. N3 even tighter.

Overall, given the visibility on the top line of the company due to the monthly reporting, I believe eyes will be glued to the bottom line. The Street expects EPS to come in at 2.66, representing a decent 37% yoy improvement at the midpoint.

Process Node Roadmap

Next year, I see two important catalysts in TSMC's roadmap, which have been reiterated during the last earnings call. I'm talking about the N2 and A16 process nodes.

CEO C. C. Wei confirmed that N2 is well on track for high volume manufacturing in the second half of 2025. From what I'm reading, the company expects a ramp profile similar to the current 3nm FinFET technology. Overall, we should see revenue flowing from this new process node in H1 2026, likely from earlier tape outs, with Q2 more likely than Q1, in my view. However, I wouldn't expect the new process node to take the first place in the charts below any time soon. Why?

Let's take a closer look at the most important AI chips planned for next year (the meat of the bone, so to speak): Nvidia's Vera-Rubin and AMD's MI4xx series.

Nvidia's Rubin (GPU) and Vera (CPU) are already taped out at TSMC using the 3 nm technology, not the newer N2. Therefore, the deployment next year will not carry any upside for TSMC's N2 process node. Looking at the Rubin Ultra (planned for 2027), there’s some speculation about the N2, or even jumping to A16 later; however, nothing has been officially confirmed.

On AMD's side, now that the company has gained relevance in the GPU space after the 6GW OpenAI deal, management announced H2 2026 MI450 deployments as part of this deal. However, AMD has not disclosed the node of the MI4xx series. Given TSMC’s N2P (a performance enhanced N2 that keeps nearly the same design rules to ease migration) hits high volume manufacturing in H2 2026, it’s possible that later MI4xx iterations (possibly the MI450X) could end up on N2P. However, as of today, there’s no confirmation, so I'm assuming no revenue from these two massive deployments. As a side note, TSMC has in its roadmap the N2X (planned for 2027), which is an even higher-performing version of the N2P, with an emphasis on HPC.

I believe the meat of the bone in 2nm technology will likely come from the N2P, as it features +18% more speed and –36% power vs N3E, and approximately +5% more speed vs N2 at iso-conditions. As for the N2X, even though it is an upgraded version of the N2P, I believe the fabless chipmakers will likely opt for the A16. According to the last update, the A16 (1.6 nm logic node) will start production in H2 2026, earlier than the N2X. Furthermore, this chip is the first TSMC node to bring backside power into production. What does this mean? Power efficiency, which is a big USP (unique selling point) for hyperscalers, which are currently constrained by power.

And when we talk about A16, we have another power efficiency improvement close to 20%. That's a big value for all the AI data center applications.

So far, the only confirmed chips on the N2 node are AMD's EPYIC Venice, the very first HPC product brought up on TSMC N2, on track for next year. Outside of the HPC/data center space, MediaTek has recently confirmed that its first 2 nm (N2P) chipset for smartphones will arrive in late 2026. Will this be enough to put the N2 in the top position of TSMC's revenue by node technology? I believe the answer is no, at least for the next couple of years. Maybe the later generations of GPUs, like the Feynman (planned for 2028), could be built on the N2 (likely the N2P) technology. Until then, I believe 3nm remains the standard for AI data center GPUs.

Looking at the Street's projections for next year, I see double-digit growth up until Q2 next year, after which it seems that growth kicks in again, likely driven by the mass production of the Vera-Rubin and the MI4xx series (on the 3nm node).

Valuation

Even after the 27% jump in the share price since my last coverage, I still see a cheap company with a strong narrative. On a P/CF basis, investors are only paying 20x for next year's operating cash flows. On a P/E basis, I see a forward multiple of 30x, still below the sector median.

I believe we could see a further multiple expansion going into 2026, driven by the new family of GPUs from Nvidia and AMD. As I've shown in the previous section, growth is likely to accelerate again in the second half of next year. That said, there is a new risk to my bull case that I want to integrate. It's called Intel.

Risks

Since the US government took a stake of 10% in Intel, Nvidia followed with an interesting partnership with the US-based chipmaker. Nvidia invested $5B in Intel and announced a multi-year plan to co-develop custom data center and PC products.

While I am not going to speculate here whether the deal would have occurred if the US government didn't have a stake in Intel, particularly as Nvidia still relies on the US government to export chips (H20) to China, I am going to flag a risk for TSMC.

On October 1, 2025, sources reported that Intel is in early-stage talks to add AMD as a foundry customer. That's not good news for TSMC (or AMD, as I discussed in my recent coverage), as the company could lose business from the second most important GPU chipmaker (fabless) in the world. So far, nothing has been confirmed, which adds uncertainty to the matter. If AMD closes a deal with Intel to become a foundry customer, I believe analysts will revise downwards TSMC's growth expectations. As to how much, I don't know (and I believe no one does), which makes it a big risk to monitor.

Conclusion

Overall, I remain bullish on TSMC.

While gross margins are under pressure as the company is expanding abroad and developing its newer N2 and A16 process nodes, I believe the top-line growth plays a more fundamental role in the narrative of this stock. As noted by the monthly sales data, TSMC is still in a healthy position, with an impressive 31.4% yoy growth reported for the month of September.

I believe the N2P node will be accretive to the top line of the company moving into 2027 and beyond. Near term, I believe the focus is still on the 3nm process node, with management reiterating that demand will remain tight for the next couple of years. As for Intel, I am not worried at the moment. However, if AMD officially confirms to be a customer of its foundry, I will begin to sweat. In the meantime, TSMC remains a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Matador Resources: Projected Free Cash Flow Still Looks Solid At Current Strip Prices
data.description:
Matador Resources (NYSE:MTDR) has generated good production results recently, but is essentially unhedged on oil (other than oil collars with a $52 floor for 2H 2025).

Despite that, it still appears capable of generating close to $5 per share (over $600 million) in 2026 free cash flow at high-$50s WTI oil. At my long-term oil price of $70, Matador may be able to generate $8+ per share in free cash flow.

I am trimming Matador's estimated value from $72 to $69 to reflect the impact of high-$50s (instead of mid-$60s) oil on its free cash flow. My outlook for oil prices beyond 2026 remains at $70, though, and Matador's financial situation at high-$50s oil in 2026 still remains pretty good.

Notes On Hedges

Matador has a relatively modest amount of hedges. It has 2H 2025 oil collars with a floor of $52, so those hedges don't have any value unless oil prices fall by another $6 to $7 this year. It is unhedged on oil in 2026 at last report.

Matador also has natural gas collars for 2026 with a wide range between the $3.50 floor and $6.70 ceiling. The spring 2026 natural gas strip is around $3.50 currently (although the full year average is around $3.90), so a further drop in prices might result in several million in natural gas hedge value for 2026.

On the other hand, Matador's Waha basis differential hedges are much weaker in 2026 (at an average of negative $2.52). Current expectations are for Waha differentials to be around negative $3 in early 2026 but improving to around negative $1 by late 2026.

Potential 2026 Outlook

I am going to model Matador's 2026 production at 200,000 BOEPD, including 117,000 barrels per day in oil production. This is roughly a 1% decline compared to its expected 2H 2025 production.

Current 2026 strip is around $58 to $59 WTI oil and $3.95 NYMEX natural gas. At those prices, Matador may generate $3.229 billion in revenues after hedges. Matador's hedges for 2026 have negative value due to its Waha basis hedges at a negative $2.52 differential.

Type

Barrels/Mcf

$ Per Barrel/Mcf

$ Million

Oil

42,705,000

$58.50

$2,498

Natural Gas

181,770,000

$536

$175

Hedge Value

Total

$3,229

If Matador can achieve these 2026 production results with $1.4 billion in total capex (within a couple percent of 2025 levels), then it would generate around $614 million in free cash flow.

This also assumes no cash income taxes due to the weak oil prices.

Expenses

Cash Taxes

$0

Total Expenditures

$2,615

Thus despite sub-$60 oil, Matador could still generate over $600 million (or close to $5 per share) in free cash flow during 2026.

Inventory Notes

Matador claimed 1,667 net operated Delaware Basin locations at the end of 2024.

It intends to turn 106.3 net operated wells to sales in 2025 (operated and non-operated), so its end of 2025 inventory at that pace would be a bit under 15 years.

However, around 26% of its inventory is in intervals (Brushy Canyon, Avalon, 2nd Bone Spring Carbonate and Wolfcamp D), where it has relatively limited development activity.

Those intervals account for 4% of its gross wells (both operated and non-operated) turned to sales in the 12 month period ending June 2025.

Excluding locations in those intervals would reduce Matador's inventory to around 10 to 11 years in its main development intervals.

Estimated Valuation

I am reducing my estimate of Matador's value from $72 per share down to $69 per share. This is based on current strip prices until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

The reduction is due to the impact on Matador's Q4 2025 and 2026 free cash flow from oil prices dropping into the high-$50s. As mentioned before, Matador doesn't have oil hedges that offer any protection at those prices.

However, despite the weaker oil prices, Matador can still generate a decent amount of free cash flow in 2026. At $70 WTI oil, it may be able to generate over $8 per share in free cash flow before cash income taxes.

Conclusion

Matador appears well positioned to deal with oil prices dropping below $60 despite its effective lack of oil hedges. At current high-$50s strip in 2026, it may still be able to generate over $600 million in free cash flow.

Matador's next note maturity is in 2028, and in the current environment, it looks capable of getting a sub-7% interest rate on new unsecured notes.

Since I take a longer-term view expecting $70 WTI oil prices, Matador looks relatively undervalued now, and I estimate its value at $69 per share (assuming a rebound to $70 oil in 2027).

data.title: Twin Disc: Diversification And End-Market Recoveries Have Driven A Strong Rebound
data.description:
Calling a bottom for small, virtually uncovered industrials is tricky, and particularly so when they serve cyclical markets where there isn’t a lot of visibility into order trends. Such was the case when I last wrote about Twin Disc (TWIN), and the shares have more than doubled since then on a faster-than-expected recovery in key end-markets, as well as some positive incremental developments in margins and diversification efforts.

With the roughly 110% movement in the share price since that last update, Twin Disc has dramatically outperformed the broader industrial sector, as well as representatives of major end markets like Caterpillar (CAT), Oshkosh (OSK), REV Group (REVG), and Sanlorenzo (OTCPK:SNLRF). Moreover, management’s initial commentary on the outlook for fiscal 2026 has been positive, and the double-digit growth in backlog and growth opportunities in defense are all encouraging.

The biggest question I still have about Twin Disc in reference to its value and attractiveness as a new buy today is whether management can drive meaningful improvements in margins and free cash flow generation. Low double-digit EBITDA margins and mid-to-high single-digit free cash flow margins should be possible, and would support a mid-to-high-teens fair value today, but I’m still not sold on the path to those levels.

An Inflection On Stronger Underlying Demand

Twin Disc shares had started moving before fiscal fourth quarter results (the most recent reported results), and I believe healthy/improving outlooks from yacht manufacturers like Beneteau (OTCPK:BTEAF) and Sanlorenzo and vocational vehicle manufacturers like Oshkosh and REV Group (in areas like aircraft, rescue, and firefighting, or ARFF) played an important role there.

Both Oshkosh and REVG have been reporting strong sales growth on pricing and volumes, and both companies have likewise seen ongoing growth in backlogs (10% growth for the Vocational business at Oshkosh in the last quarter) as healthy municipal budgets continue to drive fleet refreshment and expansion.

As far as the yacht business goes, while the first half of 2025 wasn’t particularly good for the industry, orders have rebounded strongly as the year has gone on, setting the stage for stronger results in 2026 as those orders are delivered.

Fiscal fourth quarter results drove the shares up roughly a third, with the market reacting very positively to encouraging commentary and backlog growth, as well as more clarity on the near-term growth opportunities in markets like defense.

Revenue rose more than 14% as reported, though it declined 8% in organic terms, with reported growth in all three businesses, including 13% organic growth in the Industrial segment. Unfortunately, management doesn’t provide organic segment growth information on a regular basis, but the Industrial business was clearly the strongest.

Gross margin improved 130bp year over year (to 31%) on better mix, and operating margin was steady (operating income up about 14%), while EBITDA declined 40% on foreign currency losses.

Backlog growth was one of the most positive takeaways for the report, with backlog growing 12% from both the year-ago and prior quarters, and defense growing especially strongly.

Positive Trends Across Most Markets

As I said above, the outlook for yacht manufacturers has improved significantly since early in 2025, and I would expect an incrementally more stable outlook for tariffs and international trade to be positive for the market. Moreover, with the U.S. stock market performing strongly, as well as many foreign markets, that too should be a tailwind for high-ticket luxury products into 2026.

Likewise, demand for ARFF vehicles has remained quite healthy. This has helped offset weakness in land-based energy (fracking) and some categories like agriculture and construction. Of note in energy, Twin Disc saw its first order for its e-frac offerings, and this remains a potential source of future growth, as does normalization in energy overall – while the oil/gas market is unlikely to go back to generating 25% or more of Twin Disc’s revenue, management does believe it can move back from the 8% or so in fiscal 2025 to the mid-teens that was more typical in the years before.

I’ve been more surprised by the strength Twin Disc seems to be seeing in workboats. While newbuilds in sectors like energy and shipping (barges and tugs vs. oceangoing vessels) haven’t been good, there’s been healthy demand for maintaining current fleets.

Last and by no means least is defense, and this is becoming a more interesting opportunity for the business. Patrol boats have historically been an important category for the company, and this area has remained strong, but Twin Disc has also benefitted from newer opportunities like unmanned patrol craft for the U.S. Navy. On top of this, Twin Disc is leveraged to ongoing growth in NATO spending, with the company’s Land-Based business leveraged to military vehicles like trucks.

The Outlook

Management has maintained its target of $500M in revenue for 2030, and while I find that an ambitious target, I don’t think it’s necessarily unreasonable. Acquisitions have boosted mid-single-digit underlying growth into the high single-digits across the last decade, and I do expect the company to continue identifying value-additive M&A opportunities to boost what I expect will be a mid-single-digit underlying growth rate.

Margin leverage remains the big unknown. As I’ve said before in this article and in prior articles on Twin Disc, margin leverage has long been an issue for the company and a meaningful impediment to long-term share price performance. With increasing leverage to higher-margin hybrid and electrical systems, as well as an ongoing focus from management on operational efficiency, I do think improvement is possible, but I’m reluctant to model exceptional improvement here.

But as was the case with the timing of the end-market turnarounds that helped fuel the share price spike this year, this is a case where a few strong quarters (and management commentary suggesting further improvement) could quickly shift the tone here.

As is, I do think a 10% EBITDA margin is possible over the next few years, and I don’t think a mid-teens EBITDA margin is impossible. Likewise, while I’m modeling gradual improvement in free cash flow margins to the 4% to 5% range (well above the 1% long-term average), there could yet be some upside here.

Between discounted free cash flow and an EV/EBITDA approach using a 7x multiple based upon the company’s likely 12-month operating margin and ROIC, I believe fair value for the shares is in the mid-teens today, with some upside into the higher teens on improved visibility into sustained margin improvements.

The Bottom Line

Unfortunately I missed the big move in Twin Disc shares while waiting for evidence of end-market and margin improvement. Fiscal fourth quarter results were enough to reassure the Street and the outlooks for luxury yachts, defense, and ARFF are better now, but there’s still work to be done on margins. Given my assessment of the likely trajectory of growth and margins, I consider this a hold now and a name to consider on pullbacks, as I do still have my concerns about the cyclicality and inherent profitability of the business even with attractive opportunities in areas like defense.

data.title: Winter Is Coming? Firms' Cost And Price Expectations In The Wake Of Tariff Hikes
data.description:
By Kevin Foster | Ty McClure | Brent Meyer | Daniel Weitz

A striking feature of this year's tariffs has been their (apparently) subdued impact on US retail price growth to date. Indeed, core goods prices (which exclude food and energy) have risen at an annualized rate of roughly 2 percent since the tariff announcements in early April. For comparison, the annualized growth rate in core goods prices was slightly negative during the long pre-pandemic expansion (July 2009–February 2020). And—although there might be a few reasons for the relatively small tariff passthrough onto consumer prices (including pauses between tariff announcement and enactment, which allowed many firms to build their inventories)—this lack of an initial surge in prices has prompted renewed debate over whether inflationary fears from tariffs were overblown.

From our perspective, an important place to look for answers to that debate ultimately rests with businesses—the price-setters in the economy. To that end, in this blog post, we investigate cost and price expectations from the Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations (BIE) survey and The CFO Survey (a quarterly survey of CFOs that the Atlanta Fed conducts in partnership with the Richmond Fed and Duke University). As a preview, firms overall anticipate elevated price growth over the coming year, despite some softening in the trajectory of unit cost growth. Importantly, even firms that source all their inputs domestically (and are thus less directly exposed to tariffs) have ratcheted up their price growth expectations relative to this time last year.

Around two-thirds of respondents to these surveys imported at least some portion of their inputs/supplies from abroad, while the rest source all their inputs domestically, as figure 1 shows. We compare these two groups to see if importing firms—which are more exposed to tariffs—expect meaningfully different outcomes for their business.

Figure 1: Percent of Respondents Importing Inputs/Supplies

In both the BIE and CFO surveys, average unit cost growth expectations have edged lower in the months since the tariff announcements in early April (figure 2), but they remain elevated relative to last year (before tariffs were implemented). Interestingly, unit cost expectations from domestic-sourcing (nonimporting) firms in the BIE have softened materially in September, a dynamic we have not yet detected in the CFO survey (which was last in the field between August 18 and September 5), a disparity likely resulting from differing time horizons over which questions are asked. The BIE survey elicits expectations over a 12-month-ahead horizon, while The CFO Survey gathers them for the current and next calendar years.

Figure 2: Unit Cost Expectations by Input/Supply Source

Like unit cost expectations, average price growth expectations across both surveys remain elevated relative to last year. Most worrisome is that expectations among nonimporting firms have risen sharply (figure 3). The fact that firms not exposed to tariffs expect to accelerate price increases suggests some risk that inflationary pressures are broadening beyond those firms and industries directly affected by tariffs (a finding consistent with other recent Atlanta Fed research).

Figure 3: Price Expectations by Input/Supply Source

Digging into the distribution of price growth expectations, while the means across both surveys showed some modest softening relative to the expectations that prevailed in April/May, this softening appears to be driven by firms in the upper tail of the distribution (that is, firms at or above the 90th percentile in the cross-section). For example, in The CFO Survey, the 90th percentile for 2025 price growth expectations during the second quarter (fielded between May 19 and June 6) was 15 percent. In the third quarter survey (fielded between August 18 and September 5), the 90th percentile had ebbed to 10 percent. This moderate tightening of the upper tail of the distribution might reflect some reduced uncertainty over tariffs following the Administration's announcements that took effect in early August, which may have assuaged some fears about the potential for more extreme outcomes.

There's another possible explanation for the slight reduction in expectations for price and cost growth this year relative to earlier in 2025: some firms might anticipate a more prolonged pass-through of cost increases into prices, spilling more significantly into 2026. Since the first quarter—and to a greater extent than nonimporting firms—importing firms have steadily increased their expectations for unit cost and price growth in 2026 (figure 4). This finding suggests that some of the modestly reduced unit cost and price growth expectations we observed this year might instead reflect cost and price growth that will materialize next year.

Figure 4: Firms' Expected Unit Cost and Price Growth in 2026 from The CFO Survey 1Q25 | 2Q25 | 3Q25

The impact of tariffs on consumer prices has been surprisingly muted. The standard thinking regarding tariffs is that they represent a one-time shift in the overall price level, but as Chair Powell noted in a speech last month, “A 'one-time' increase does not mean 'all at once.' Tariff increases will likely take some time to work their way through supply chains.” Indeed, the lack of immediate passthrough might be related to gaps between the announcement and imposition of new tariffs, uncertainty over where tariff rates will ultimately land, or the choice by importing firms to respond to tariff-related cost increases by squeezing their own profit margins rather than increasing prices to customers.

Responses to our business surveys suggest a delayed—but nevertheless meaningful—impact from tariffs. Firms' unit cost and price growth expectations have risen markedly since this time last year, with prices expected to remain elevated into 2026. At the same time, even nonimporting firms that are not directly exposed to increased tariff rates expect an acceleration in price growth, suggesting some broadening of price pressures.

Ample uncertainty over the trajectory of price growth remains and may ultimately hinge on whether the strength in demand is sufficient to weather the impact of higher price growth. If demand softens, firms may be dealing with squeezed margins instead.

This places a policymaker in a position similar to a weather forecaster. The forecaster, when faced with an unseasonably mild October and November, must assess if the mild weather will continue or if weather patterns will revert to more serious winter dynamics. Similarly, a central banker, when faced with mild price changes, must assess if that mildness will persist or if more significant changes may be on the horizon. Firms responding to our surveys—one input a central banker would consider—anticipate that the mild weather is unlikely to last. Their forecast is that winter is coming.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Newmont: Gold Supercycle Continues
data.description:
Executive Summary

There are various reasons to remain bullish on Newmont Corporation (NYSE:NEM) and to reiterate a 'Buy' rating for the stock. The management remains laser focused on delivering value for NEM's shareholders. This is evident in the way the team is reshaping the company by shedding its less profitable assets and redirecting capital toward more promising projects. Furthermore, the macroeconomic environment is particularly favorable for precious metals right now. Gold prices are rising amid growing economic uncertainty and quite aggressive target price upgrades for this precious metal. Additionally, the valuation is extremely attractive as the forward P/E is expected to shrink dramatically. Last but not least, the previous NEM write-up has aged well because the stock has gained almost 35% since early July, indicating strong share price momentum.

Fundamental Analysis

As always, let’s start by looking at the bigger picture. Gold thrives in uncertainty—and that’s exactly the sort of environment we are in right now. The Federal Reserve has started cutting interest rates, but Chair Jerome Powell remains cautious about future cuts, stating they are likely to be gradual. Given that the Fed tends to be fairly conservative when easing monetary policy, I believe this gradual move to be true. The “higher for longer” approach to interest rates will keep borrowing costs elevated, putting pressure on corporate spending and household demand. As economic growth slows, so the unemployment rate will climb, increasing the chance of a recession. That’s when investors look for safety—and gold has always been a safe haven during rocky times.

Looking beyond the U.S., China’s economy is struggling with rising U.S. trade tariffs and an aging population. The Eurozone isn’t faring much better either. Trade surpluses are at decade-long lows due to U.S. tariffs and declining global demand, while geopolitical uncertainty from Ukraine to global trade tensions will also push investors to seek a more stable store of value.

It’s no surprise, then, that analysts are turning increasingly bullish on gold prices. Analysts at Goldman Sachs recently upgraded their target price for gold to $4,900/oz. In short, as the world’s largest gold producer, Newmont now has the perfect macro environment to thrive in.

Zooming in on the company itself, we can see that the fundamentals are rock solid. The operating leverage is gaining momentum with gross and operating margins expanding thanks to the company’s strict cost management and efficiency gains. As demand for gold rises, Newmont is capturing more profit per ounce, which is a clear sign of strong execution.

The management team has also been particularly active in capital allocation. Over the past year, the company has sold stakes in Orla Mining, the Coffee Project, Ghana mines and Orosur Mining. These moves align perfectly with the company’s strategic pivot to focus its efforts on higher-yield, lower-cost projects.

As a result, the company’s balance sheet has strengthened considerably and is now ready for new investments. The tangible value per share has increased by more than 20% over the last four quarters, marking a massive improvement. Its net debt has fallen by over $5 billion during the same period, while the total debt is not much higher than the total cash.

Some investors might view the recent management shakeup as a risk, but I think it looks both smooth and strategic. The new CEO, Natascha Viljoen, is not someone from the outside. She served as the former COO during the company’s turnaround phase, which started in 2023. Given that the turnaround is well underway, to me her promotion is a sign of continuity, not disruption.

Valuation Analysis

I believe the valuation is one of the most bullish aspects of Newmont’s investment case. The company’s forward P/E ratio is expected to decline sharply from 24.49 in 2024 to just 11.25 by 2028. This downward trajectory means investors are paying less for each dollar of expected earnings as the company’s profits strengthen.

And if we compare Newmont’s current P/E ratios to historical levels, it is significantly cheaper, and therefore significantly undervalued. The company’s current non-GAAP forward P/E is around 15, so roughly 20% below its 5-year average of 18.7. In other words, it seems that the market still hasn’t fully priced in the improved earnings to be gained from the company’s recent efforts of optimizing its assets and focusing on higher-margin production.

So, while the share price has risen, earnings expectations have risen faster, leaving plenty of room for a further upside as the management continues its optimization program.

Mitigating Factors

Firstly, despite current valuation ratios and the forward P/E ratio suggesting that NEM is attractively valued, Wall Street analysts clearly believe that all the optimism has already been priced in. In fact, Wall Street’s average target price for Newmont is around $89, implying very limited further upside. And without a more positive sentiment from Wall Street, investors might be less keen to invest.

Another factor worth considering is that Newmont operates across multiple geographies and complex environments. Mining is by nature an industry prone to certain unpredictable challenges such as equipment failures, harsh weather conditions, or even natural disasters, all of which can quickly impact production schedules. Any prolonged halts to operations or budget overruns would undoubtedly affect near-term performance.

Finally, the biggest wild card is the sheer volatility of gold prices. Even though, as mentioned, the current macro climate favors gold demand, this situation won’t last forever. All it will take is for inflation to rise faster than expected or for the global economy to bounce back, and investors will return to risk assets, a shift that would undoubtedly lead to a decline in gold demand, and thus impact Newmont’s earnings.

Conclusion

Newmont is clearly a world-class gold producer poised to benefit from the uncertain macro environment. Its fundamentals are extremely strong, with manageable debt and a solid cash pile. Meanwhile, the management is busy streamlining operations to ensure the company is poised to benefit from the upcoming gold supercycle. And even after a 35% gain since July, the valuation remains incredibly attractive, with earnings outpacing share price, and forward P/E ratios expected to fall quite dramatically over the next few years.

data.title: Government Shutdown: What Investors May See In The Market
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Global Market Strategist

The federal government’s fifth shutdown this century has entered its second week. As Congress continues to disagree over funding, large parts of the US government sit dormant. The market, so far, hasn’t seemed to care. What is a government shutdown, and when will it be resolved? How has the government shutdown affected the stock market? Should investors be concerned?

Shutdowns limit government operations

During the shutdown, essential government functions have continued. This has included, but hasn’t been limited to, air-traffic control, border patrol, federal prisons, power grids, mail delivery, disaster relief, food-safety inspections, military, and tax collection. Seniors have continued to receive Social Security and Medicare. Veteran benefits, unemployment benefits, and food stamps have been administered.

Many non-essential government activities have ceased, though. Examples abound, but here are just a few. The Justice Department has suspended many civil cases. NASA has paused most projects but continues to support the International Space Station. Certain national parks have remained open, but with limited hours and staffing. The National Zoo has continued to operate using last year’s funds but is set to close Oct. 12 (though the animals will be fed.)

A shutdown is no laughing matter for the estimated 3 million federal employees. Non-essential workers have been furloughed. Essential workers may be paid retroactively when Congress funds the government again. In 2019, roughly 800,000 non-essential workers were furloughed, and 1.3 million essential workers received IOUs.

Government shutdowns tend to resolve quickly

Government shutdowns have precedent. This is the 22nd since modern Congressional budgeting began in 1976. The longest — 34 days — lasted from December 2018 to January 2019, during the first Trump administration.1 Most were resolved much more quickly, with an average length of eight days. Five only lasted a day.

Shutdowns typically end when one or both political parties can no longer withstand the public’s ire. During the 2013 government shutdown, Congress’s approval rating fell to a record-low 9%.2 For context, at the time, that was below approval ratings for head lice, root canals, traffic jams, and even colonoscopies. As of September 2025, 26% of Americans approved of Congress, down from a multi-year high of 36% in March 2021.3

Government shutdowns have had minimal impact on the stock market

The good news is that government shutdowns haven’t really affected the stock market historically. While there have been examples of heightened market volatility, it’s generally been benign. (And so far that’s been true of this shutdown as well.) The S&P 500 Index posted positive returns during 12 of the 21 previous government shutdowns. The average S&P 500 return during those shutdowns was 0.1%.4 (Remember, shutdowns have been resolved, on average, within eight days.)

Not all government shutdowns created market volatility

In the 12 months following the December 2018 government shutdown — the most recent — the S&P 500 Index gained 26.2%. It returned 19.72% in the year following the infamous shutdown of 2013. In fact, since 1980, the average S&P 500 return in the 12 months following the government closing is 16.95%.5

Extending the timeline even further, a $100,000 investment in the S&P 500 Index in 1957 would’ve been worth $12.6 million by the end of 2024.6 That’s despite 21 government shutdowns.

Investors should consider sticking to long-term investing plans

While unnerving, concerns about shutdowns shouldn’t change investors’ long-term investment plans; sticking to the plan may be the best way forward. This isn't the first government shutdown, and it likely won’t be the last. We expect the spending bill (or bills) to pass eventually and government operations to return to normal.

In the meantime, the political gamesmanship continues. Past shutdowns have generally been short with little noticeable impact for most people. Let’s hope history repeats itself.

Footnotes

  1. Source: US Treasury, 8/31/23

  2. Source: Gallup, 2/26/25. The Congressional Approval Rating fell to 5% in the Associated Press poll. It never got below 9% in the Gallup poll.

  3. Source: Gallup, 9/16/25

  4. Source: Bloomberg, L.P. 12/31/22. An investment cannot be made into an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
  5. Source: Bloomberg L.P., 2/26/25, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. An investment cannot be made into an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.
  6. Source: Bloomberg L.P., 12/19/24. The S&P 500 Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index of the 500 largest domestic US stocks. Performance based on price returns. An investment cannot be made into an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Important Information

NA4891474

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be purchased directly by investors. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only and does not predict or depict the performance of any investment.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Oct. 10, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Government shutdown: What investors may see in the market by Invesco US

data.title: Lumentum Holdings: A Rising Star In Optical Technology Worth Your Investment
data.description:
Lumentum Holdings (NASDAQ:LITE), a market-leading designer and manufacturer of optical and photonic products that enable high-speed communications and advanced laser applications worldwide, has had a solid year so far, with a year-to-date increase of 92.28%.

Most of that outperformance has come over the last six months, driven by robust demand for its optical products from the proliferation of artificial intelligence ("AI") and cloud computing.

Training and using AI models in production environments (especially large language models) and cloud computing require massive, high-speed, low-latency data transfers for many applications. Since fiber optics provides significantly higher speeds and lower latency than copper cabling for data transmission, it has become the preferred technology for transferring data in AI infrastructure and modern data centers.

Lumentum manufactures the three most popular types of lasers used in fiber optic networks: Directly Modulated Lasers (DMLs), Electro-Absorption-Modulated Lasers (EMLs), and Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Lasers (VCSELs). You may hear these lasers discussed in company communications.

Network engineers primarily use VCSELs for short-distance, high-bandwidth multimode (multiple light paths) fiber links, such as high-bandwidth links from servers to top-of-rack (ToR) switches, connecting different components within the same equipment rack, and up to 100-meter interconnects between switches located in different racks.

Lower-speed, shorter-distance (up to 10 km) applications use DML. Network engineers utilize DML within a data center for cost-effective, low-power, short- to medium-distance optical interconnections between servers, switches, and storage systems to transfer massive amounts of data. DML is ideal for applications like high-performance computing (HPC) clusters and storage area networks (SANs), which require low-latency links over short distances.

Higher-speed, long-distance applications, such as fiber optics between data centers, that require a "cleaner" and more stable signal use EMLs.

The company also manufactures other products beyond lasers, including coherent transceivers and ROADM (Reconfigurable Optical Add-Drop Multiplexers) Modules, which enhance data transfer efficiency, flexibility, and scalability for telecom, metro, long-haul, and submarine (undersea) networks. The company introduced its coherent transceivers to the market around 2021 and its initial ROADM products around 2016. I won't delve too deeply into the specifics of what these products do in this article. I will perhaps discuss them in future articles. Currently, it's sufficient to know that these products have helped the company to achieve an exceptional financial performance over the last six months.

More recently, the company has released its R64 Optical Circuit Switches ("OCS"), a product management expects will be a future revenue growth driver. Network engineers use OCS to establish high-speed, light-based connections between different fibers by reflecting light beams, often using tiny mirrors, without converting the signal to electrical form.

Speaking of revenue, in its latest quarter, Lumentum reported fourth-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2025 revenue of $480.7 million, above the high end of its revised guidance range $440 million to $470 million. The areas investors are most interested in, specifically the Cloud & Networking Segment, drove most of its total revenue growth, with the segment's fourth-quarter revenue increasing 16% sequentially and 67% year-over-year, led by hyperscale cloud customer demand. Due to the strength of these results, management has issued total revenue guidance for the first quarter of FY 2026 of $510 million to $540 million. If the company meets the midpoint of its first-quarter guidance, it would set a new all-time quarterly revenue record.

The best part is that the company's revenue is translating into improved margins and a growing bottom line. Its fourth quarter FY 2025 non-GAAP (generally accepted accounting principles) operating margin increased 420 basis points ("bps") sequentially and over 2,000 bps year-over-year, primarily due to improved Cloud & Networking profitability.

You may notice that the company still has negative GAAP operating margins.

The main reason for its negative GAAP operating margins is due to several acquisitions, including IPG Photonics' (IPGP) telecom transmission business, and NeoPhotonics in August 2022, as well as Cloud Light Technology in November 2023. Although the bulk of one-time restructuring and integration costs are in the past, the company may still recognize some integration-related expenses, plant closures, or severance costs under GAAP. Lumentum still incurs Amortization of Intangible Assets from those acquisitions, totaling $34.6 million in the fourth quarter. When you add acquisition-related costs to the company's stock-based compensation of $40 million, the company's GAAP operating loss is $8.4 million.

However, the company's core profitability (non-GAAP operating profits) has grown rapidly over the last year. Additionally, the market anticipates GAAP profitability soon, given the company's positive non-GAAP operating margin guidance.

The company has forecasted a non-GAAP first quarter FY 2026 operating margin of 16–17.5%. This upward trend in non-GAAP metrics from 15% in the fourth quarter of FY2025 suggests that GAAP profitability is likely in the near future. However, since the market has likely already factored in future GAAP operating margins, don't expect GAAP profitability to be a catalyst for further upside.

The company reported non-GAAP diluted earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.52, which exceeded the high end of the company's guidance.

Since some may consider the stock overvalued based on some valuation metrics, is it still worthwhile to invest in it today? The answer is yes for aggressive growth investors. The company continues to innovate, and management has planned to begin sampling a product that is expected to have a significant impact on Lumentum's revenue and market position soon. Therefore, the company has a solid catalyst for future upside ahead.

A next-generation approach

One product I would like to discuss is Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) with ultra-high-power lasers. CPO is the next-generation approach to enhancing computing performance by integrating VCSELs into a separate optical engine that is then co-packaged with a switch ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) on the same substrate. This arrangement significantly reduces the electrical path between the laser and switch ASIC compared to traditional pluggable optics, making the switch more energy-efficient.

Lumentum is developing CPO in collaboration with NVIDIA (NVDA), a partnership that is central to NVIDIA's effort to develop energy-efficient network switches for "AI factories." A Lumentum March 18, 2025 press release stated: "Scaling AI factories presents significant power consumption challenges. NVIDIA's newly announced Spectrum-X Photonics and Quantum-X Photonics networking switches integrate photonic innovations, including Lumentum's high-power, high-efficiency lasers, to significantly reduce power consumption, enhance resilience, and enable faster deployment." Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Michael E. Hurlston said on the fourth quarter earnings call that he expects "a significant revenue ramp in CPO by the second half of calendar 2026." He also stated:

Today, our components business is a key foundation of our success, and we are well positioned for significant growth in our cloud modules business and to lead in emerging technologies like OCS and CPO. As we scale production, expand capacity and intensify our focus on high-growth, high-margin opportunities, we are confident in our ability to deliver continued top line growth, margin expansion and long-term shareholder value.

If OCS and CPO pan out for the company, the stock price may still increase from here, despite some valuation measures flashing red.

Valuation

Lumentum Holdings' trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 6.797, which is well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, suggesting overvaluation or that the market expects future revenue growth, or that the quality of revenue will improve (increased margins). If the stock were to trade at its five-year median, the price would be $81.70, representing a 48% decline from its October 7 closing price of $157.15.

The following table compares Lumentum's 2026 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) and TTM profitability metrics to several of its peers in the laser industry.

Company Name 2026 Forward P/S ratio 2026 Forward estimated revenue growth 2026 Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Lumentum Holdings 6.10 39.53% 0.15 -6.36% 23.89% -10.97% 0.73%
Coherent (COHR) 3.19 7.92% 0.40 3.31% 36.08% 10.42% 0.25%
IPG Photonics 3.69 7.27% 0.51 6.83% 33.81% -0.71% -10.12%%%
Viavi Solutions (VIAV) 2.38 10.00% 0.24 6.20% 56.84% 5.38% 2.48%
Marvell (MRVL) 9.14 40.95% 0.22 21.14% 44.64% 6.02% -0.57%
Ciena (CIEN) 3.95 16.98% 0.23 13.33% 38.76% 5.60% 3.19%
Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) 2.39 55.88% 0.04 -66.37% 28.72% -13.02% -35.64%

Typically, the market considers a company with a lower P/S/G ratio to be undervalued compared to companies with higher P/S/G ratios. Following that rationale, the market undervalues Lumentum relative to its peers, except Applied Optoelectronics. However, when comparing some of its profitability metrics, Lumentum may deserve its lower valuation compared to most of its peers. For instance, Lumentum has the second-lowest FCF margin at -6.36%. Except for Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum is also the only company with negative FCF.

Lumentum also has the lowest TTM gross margin and the second-lowest TTM operating margin. Considering all these factors, it may deserve its lower valuation, and some investors may be unwilling to award it a premium valuation compared to its peers because of its inferior FCF and profitability.

The following chart illustrates Lumentum's EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) growth, increasing by 24.19% from $593.94 million to $916.03 million.

The following table compares Lumentum's EV/EBITDA, two-year EBITDA CAGR, and TTM EBITDA margin using metrics derived from YCharts. The market factors in the EBITDA growth rate, EBITDA margin, potential sustainability of EBITDA growth, and quality of the balance sheet. Sometimes these other factors are why companies with higher EBITDA growth rates or EBITDA margins don't always have a higher EV/EBITDA valuation.

One significant issue that Lumentum has, which may be a reason investors discount its valuation in this comparison and in all other valuation metrics, is the state of its balance sheet. At the end of its fiscal fourth quarter, the company had $877.10 million in cash and short-term investments, compared to $2.608 billion in long-term debt.

Lumentum's debt-to-equity ratio (D/E) is 2.299, indicating that the company primarily funds its operations through debt rather than equity. A D/E above 2.0 may be a sign of financial risk. It also has a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 11.83. Generally, the market finds a debt-to-EBITDA ratio above 5.0 a sign of high leverage, which is a key risk for Lumentum, potentially making it difficult for the company to pay down debt if its EBITDA growth slows.

According to Cbonds, S&P Global Ratings rates Lumentum Holdings' LT credit at "B" with a negative outlook. A S&P Global rating of B is a highly speculative, non-investment-grade rating. A negative outlook means the rating is in danger of dropping to B-, which is one grade above a "substantial risks" rating.

Lumentum's GAAP TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 434.05, well above the Information Technology ("IT") sector median of 31.39, suggesting either overvaluation or that the market expects earnings to multiply over the next several years.

The company's non-GAAP TTM P/E was 78.34, above the IT sector median of 24.94, which some may consider a sign of the market overvaluing the stock.

The stock's 2026 forward P/E to estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.24, calculated by dividing its 2026 forward P/E of 33.06 by its estimated EPS growth of 135.71%. The market typically considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of an undervalued stock. Suppose it sold at a perceived fair valuation of 1.0; its price would be $659.55, a 320% rise from its October 7 closing price of $157.15.

However, although the company's PEG ratio implies a significant upside, the market may never value it at a 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0 due to analysts' and the market's skepticism of the company sustaining such high EPS growth rates, as evidenced by analysts' rapid estimated EPS growth deceleration from 135.71% in FY 2026 to 33.46% in FY 2027 and 12.90% in FY 2028. The market rarely rewards stocks with potential decelerating growth. More often, the market will discount stocks with decelerating growth. Additionally, the company's negative FCF, in concert with its balance sheet situation, may discourage the market from increasing its valuation much beyond where it is now, without a significant catalyst.

Risks

Lumentum relies on large, capital-intensive infrastructure buildouts from hyperscalers and telecom providers, which have historically exhibited significant cyclicality. The market may fear the potential downside from an inventory-driven recession/slowdown similar to the 2022–2023 post-pandemic inventory correction in hardware and the 2001 dot-com bubble burst. Occasionally, the market overinvests in new hardware technology, and when demand slackens, the bubble can burst quickly—a legitimate concern for investors. This dynamic may be another reason the market discounts the stock's valuation.

The company faces execution risks in bringing new products, such as 200G EMLs, OCS, and CPO, onto the market. Suppose Lumentum runs into manufacturing or yield delays when ramping these new products up to full production; the company's EPS may fail to reach analysts' estimates. Investors would likely sell off the stock in response.

Investors may also be accounting for the fiercely competitive optical components industry. The market may assume that competitive factors will introduce pricing pressure, resulting in a faster erosion of the new product's gross margins than Lumentum can counter through cost reductions, which could slow profit growth below expectations.

Another issue that investors may factor into the company's valuation is the company's lack of FCF; It burned $104.70 million in cash over the last year.

Much of the reason for its 2025 annual negative FCF is its increased annual capital expenditure ("CapEx"), TTM $231 million as of the fourth quarter of 2025. Management invested most of that CapEx in the company's Cloud segment. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Wajid Ali stated on the company's fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call, "In Q4, we invested $59 million in CapEx, primarily focused on manufacturing capacity to support cloud customers."

Although the company generated a positive FCF of $10.10 million in the fourth quarter, it still spends significant funds on CapEx. Management may still need to increase CapEx to maintain its edge in its Cloud & Networking Segment. So, the company may continue burning cash, at least in the near to medium term. As a result, some investors may continue questioning the quality of Lumentum's revenue until the company can show steady FCF growth.

The company's lack of FCF may eventually put the company in a precarious position since its balance sheet is not in the best shape. The company could quickly run into trouble if EBITDA growth slows.

While not an immediate risk, if the company ever falls into financial distress, which investors may identify by a rising debt-to-EBITDA ratio and debt rating agencies downgrading its debt to a point where default becomes a real possibility, it would be a thesis-busting event. An example would be S&P downgrading the debt to CCC.

Lumentum is a buy for aggressive growth investors

Value or risk-averse investors are better off avoiding this stock. The company's negative cash flow, GAAP unprofitability, and questionable balance sheet may harm the company if Lumentum encounters execution risks or enters a cyclical downturn.

However, if the company's new products succeed, especially its 200G EMLs, OCS, and its future CPO collaboration with NVIDIA, the company may have a long-term revenue and EPS growth runway that could entice growth investors. This stock may be suitable for aggressive growth investors who understand when to hold (when a company is experiencing temporary difficulties) and when to sell (when a truly thesis-busting event occurs). Only investors with the mental fortitude and patience to stay invested, even when the company encounters performance hiccups, should consider investing in this stock.

Although I don't expect the stock to achieve a 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0, I believe it can trade much higher than 0.24, especially if its new products succeed and it continues improving its profitability and FCF. As a result, I rate Lumentum a buy for aggressive growth investors only.
